name: Modern Gothic Poster
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as Neo Gothic/Grotesk, drawing on early American gothics such as Franklin Gothic and Barnhart Brothers condensed gothics. It shares the double-storey g and sturdy terminals characteristic of the American grotesque tradition.
summary: A narrower, high-contrast poster cut completing the Modern Gothic trilogy, drawn from early American gothic sources including Franklin Gothic and Barnhart Brothers condensed gothics.
description: Designed by Malte Bentzen and released in 2025, it refines every curve and counter rather than simply condensing the standard Modern Gothic, resulting in oval-shaped terminals and increased stroke contrast that suit large-scale display work. Three weights — Medium, Bold and Black — each come with a matching italic, plus a variable font, and the proportions are balanced to pair with the Modern Gothic Standard and Mono siblings. Historical reference material from Barnhart Brothers & Spindler (1901) and American Type Founders (1906) underpins the design, carrying features like the double-storey g forward into a contemporary cut.
foundry_blurb: AllCaps describe Modern Gothic Poster as the final chapter in the Modern Gothic trilogy and a return to the rugged roots of American typography. Narrower than the standard cut but fully reimagined rather than merely condensed, it draws on lesser-known sources such as Modern Gothic Condensed from Barnhart Brothers & Spindler alongside celebrated classics like Franklin Gothic. The expanding terminals shift away from geometrically pure rounds towards a more condensed, oval-like shape, and the family is designed to combine harmoniously with its Standard and Mono siblings.

name: Society
classification_notes: The proportions, weight range, small caps, swash alternates and text performance suggest a transitional or humanist serif lineage, designed with both editorial display and continuous reading in mind.
summary: A text-focused serif family from AllCaps with six weights, matching italics, and a thorough OpenType feature set suited to book and editorial work.
description: Six weights from Light to Bold, each with an italic, give the family enough range for both headline and body copy. The OpenType set is unusually thorough: small caps, swash alternates, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions and several alternate letterforms for S, Q and Z. Specimen usage leans heavily into publishing contexts, from zine covers to long-form prose, suggesting a typeface designed with print production in mind.
foundry_blurb: AllCaps present Society as a versatile serif for editorial and publishing use, with a full range of OpenType features including small caps, swash characters, multiple figure styles and contextual alternates, demonstrated across display, text and caption settings.

name: Affix
classification_notes: Affix is a geometric sans-serif with alternate forms for key characters (a, g, t) and a clean, technical aesthetic with roots in keyboard and typewriter culture. Its even stroke weight and circular forms place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans with a technical, keyboard-inflected character and a thoughtful set of OpenType alternates across five weights.
description: Five weights from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic, give Affix a workable range for both display and text settings. Character alternates for the lowercase a, g, t and y, alongside round dot variants, allow for tonal shifts between humanist warmth and strict geometric rigour. The specimen leans into typewriter and computing references, from the IBM Selectric to Olivetti, suggesting an intended audience comfortable with that kind of visual shorthand.
foundry_blurb: AllCaps present Affix as a geometric sans available in five weights with italics, featuring a full set of OpenType features including alternate letterforms for a, g, t and y, proportional and tabular figures, fractions, and case-sensitive forms. The specimen references typewriter and keyboard history, including IBM and Olivetti products.

name: Visual
classification_notes: Visual is a geometric sans-serif with a technical, data-driven aesthetic. Its clean, modular letterforms and strong performance at both display and text sizes place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans in six weights with a technical character, suited to data-driven editorial work and interface design with satellite and cartography overtones.
description: Six weights from Light to Black cover a wide range of applications, from large display settings to extended body text. The OpenType feature set includes fractions, ordinals, superscript, subscript, scientific inferiors and a stylistic set, suggesting intent for scientific and technical publishing. Sample copy themed around pixels, satellite imagery and cartographic projections reinforces its positioning in technical and data-facing design work.
foundry_blurb: AllCaps present Visual as a geometric sans-serif typeface with six weights, interactive specimen previews, and an OpenType feature set that includes fractions, case-sensitive forms, localized forms, and scientific inferiors. The specimen leans heavily on technical subject matter including pixels, satellite data and cartographic projections.

name: Bandit
classification_notes: AllCaps classify it as Geometric Condensed Sans; its reversed contrast, modular construction and exclusively condensed-to-compressed width range place it firmly in the display category with roots in both Swiss modernist geometry and American wood type.
summary: A display sans by Sylvan Lanz that fuses 1930s Swiss geometric modernism with the reversed contrast of 19th-century American wood type across four condensed cuts and a variable font.
description: Designed by Sylvan Lanz and released in 2023, Bandit runs from Super-Compressed to Condensed, with the variable font spanning the full width range in a single file. Its reversed contrast, where horizontals are heavier than verticals, draws directly from North American wood type traditions while the modular construction reflects Swiss rational design of the 1930s. Three OpenType stylistic sets add Bauhaus letterforms, hairline accents and a monocase option with clipped descenders for tight leading.
foundry_blurb: AllCaps describe Bandit as the result of a happy marriage between burgeoning rationalist ideas and American wood type shapes, noting that 1930s Swiss designers drew geometric typefaces using modular systems whose inverted contrasts share features with 18th and 19th century North American wood type. They position it as a flexible, multi-condensed display typeface suited to wanted posters, headlines and section dividers, and highlight a variable font that stretches faster than its shadow.

name: Rhetorik Sans
classification_notes: A clean, neutrally proportioned sans-serif with grotesque characteristics, offering multiple stylistic alternates and a range of weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics.
summary: A clean grotesque sans from AllCaps spanning five weights with italics and a broad set of OpenType features suited to editorial and institutional use.
description: Five weights from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic, give the family reasonable range for both display and text settings. A generous OpenType feature set covers fractions, superscript, subscript, tabular figures, case-sensitive forms and multiple stylistic sets. Sample copy referencing postmodern architecture and cultural institutions suggests a positioning towards design-led editorial and cultural contexts.
foundry_blurb: AllCaps present Rhetorik Sans as a versatile sans-serif family with ten styles running from Thin to Bold with italics throughout, supported by an extensive OpenType feature set including fractions, ligatures, stylistic alternates and figure variants.

name: Rhetorik Serif
classification_notes: A contemporary serif with a broad weight range from Thin to Bold, carrying a rational, architectural character suited to editorial and identity work. The specimen copy references architecture and urban planning, pointing to a design sensibility aligned with modernist graphic traditions.
summary: A five-weight contemporary serif from AllCaps with full italic coverage and an extensive OpenType feature set, pitched at editorial and architectural contexts.
description: Running from Thin to Bold with matching italics across all five weights, it covers a wide range of typographic applications from large display settings to small running text. The specimen copy draws heavily on architectural and urban planning references, suggesting a rational, modernist sensibility in the design. Three stylistic sets and a thorough array of figure options, fractions and alternates extend its utility for complex editorial and data-driven layouts.
foundry_blurb: AllCaps present Rhetorik Serif as a contemporary serif family in ten styles, spanning Thin through Bold with italics, and equipped with a full complement of OpenType features including stylistic sets, case-sensitive forms, fractions and multiple figure styles.

name: Modern Gothic
classification_notes: A neo-grotesque with alternate single-storey 'a' and 'g' forms, extensive OpenType feature set, and a variable font, placing it squarely in the grotesque sans-serif tradition with contemporary refinements.
summary: A six-weight grotesque with matching italics, a variable font, and an unusually thorough OpenType feature set suited to editorial and information-heavy typesetting.
description: Running from Thin to Black with italic counterparts at every weight, the family includes a fully variable file covering both weight and italic axes. The OpenType palette is notably complete, covering fractions, ordinals, case-sensitive forms, tabular figures, scientific inferiors and stylistic alternates including single-storey 'a' and 'g'. Specimen copy drawn from Baudrillard and David Foster Wallace signals an editorial positioning around media, information and civic discourse.
foundry_blurb: AllCaps present Modern Gothic as a contemporary grotesque available across six weights with italics and a variable font, featuring a comprehensive OpenType feature set including alternate letterforms, figure styles, fractions and more.

name: Modern Gothic Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface with a gothic sans-serif construction, combining fixed-width spacing with a grotesque character structure suited to editorial and screen use.
summary: A monospaced gothic sans in four weights with italics, suited to editorial interfaces, data contexts and text-heavy screen work.
description: Four weights from Thin to Medium, each with a matching italic, give this fixed-width grotesque a reasonable range for both display and body settings. OpenType features include stylistic alternates for 'a', 'g' and 'J', fractions, ordinals and a full suite of numeral forms, making it usable across tables, UI and long-form text. The sample texts drawn from Baudrillard, McLuhan and David Foster Wallace suggest an intent to position the face within critical media and information design contexts.
foundry_blurb: AllCaps present Modern Gothic Mono as a fixed-width gothic sans with a comprehensive OpenType feature set including alternate letterforms, fractions, ligatures and multiple numeral styles, demonstrated across a range of sizes from large display settings down to 8px running text.

name: Youth
classification_notes: Youth draws directly from the Bauhaus-inflected phototypesetting grotesques of the late 1960s and 70s, tracing back to Herbert Bayer's Universalschrift forms. Its high x-height, rounded corners and optically corrected curves place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans drawn from the Bauhaus-inflected phototypesetting grotesques of the late 1960s and 70s, reworked with rounded corners, a high x-height and multiple stylistic alternates.
description: Taking cues from Herbert Bayer's Universalschrift and the Letraset-era work of designers such as Herb Lubalin and David L Burke, Youth reimagines that distinctive genre with optically corrected curves and dynamic rounded terminals rather than strict modularity. Six weights run from Thin to Black with matching italics, and an extensive OpenType feature set includes lightweight contrasting diacritics, contextual alternates and both kinetic and geometric alternate characters. The result suits headline and branding work where a recognisably Bauhaus flavour is wanted without the rigidity of its historical sources.
foundry_blurb: AllCaps describe Youth as a typeface that skipped class once or twice but learned well from the Bauhaus phototypesetting genre. Reimagined with dynamic rounded corners and lively proportions, it is systematic but not modular, with optically corrected curves providing a contemporary balance over its film-based predecessors. A high x-height aids use in headlines and lead paragraphs, and unexpected turns in the drawing give it distinct character. Multiple stylistic sets, including one with lightweight contrasting diacritics, plus wide-ranging alternative characters, extend its versatility for branding and identity projects.
